question_text: Two spheres, X and Y, are dropped simultaneously from the same height in a vacuum chamber where air resistance is negligible. Sphere X has a mass of 0.5 kg and sphere Y has a mass of 2 kg. Both experience the same gravitational force per unit mass (g = 10 m/s²). Using F = ma and Newton's second law, explain why both spheres hit the ground at the same time, and calculate the acceleration of each sphere.
correct_answer: Both spheres accelerate at 10 m/s². Sphere X experiences a gravitational force of 5 N (F = 0.5 × 10), and sphere Y experiences 20 N (F = 2 × 10). Using a = F/m: sphere X has a = 5/0.5 = 10 m/s² and sphere Y has a = 20/2 = 10 m/s². Since both have the same acceleration, they take the same time to fall.
explanation: Even though sphere Y is heavier and experiences a larger gravitational force, its greater mass means the same acceleration results. Newton's second law (F = ma) shows that when force increases proportionally with mass, acceleration stays constant. This is why all objects fall at the same rate in a vacuum.
